questionText: Find the maximum and minimum values of $\dfrac{1}{10 + 6\sin\theta - 8\cos\theta}$.

steps.description: Write the varying part in R-form

steps.explanation: The sine part ranges over $[-10,10]$, so the denominator ranges over $[0, 20]$.
When the sine equals $-1$, the denominator is $0$, so the fraction is undefined there and can grow without bound nearby.
As the denominator approaches $0$ from above, the fraction tends to $+\infty$, so there is no finite maximum.
The smallest positive fraction comes from the largest denominator $20$, giving $\frac{1}{20}$.
This example highlights why checking whether the denominator can vanish matters.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Minimum $= \dfrac{1}{20}$; there is no finite maximum (denominator can reach $0$).
